scene.name: The Below-Stairs, Where the Unseen Wait

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Tilda
npcs.title: who kept a great house running for fifty years, unseen
npcs.hail: *(A spare, upright old woman sits with her reddened, work-ruined hands folded in her lap, and she has the particular stillness of a person who spent a lifetime being present in rooms without ever being *noticed* in them — the trained invisibility of the very good servant, who learns to be the hands that did the thing and never the face anyone remembers. She watches you with shrewd, tired, kindly eyes, and there is in her no bitterness exactly, but a deep and weary knowledge of exactly where she stood in the order of things.)* …You'll be lost, I expect. *(a dry, warm voice)* …Folk don't *come* below-stairs on purpose; they come when they've taken a wrong turn looking for the *grand* rooms. *(a faint smile)* …I'm Tilda. Housekeeper. Fifty years I kept a great house — the Marlowe house, not that you'd know it, not that anyone *knows* it by the *housekeeper* — fifty years of fires laid before anyone woke, floors scrubbed before anyone trod them, meals on the table as if by *magic*, because the whole *art* of my trade, cold one, was to make the *work* *invisible*, so the *family* upstairs could live their *grand* lives never once *seeing* the *hundred* *hands* that *held* them *up.* *(she folds her ruined hands)* …And I was *good* at it. The *best.* …Which means, cold one, that I spent fifty years being *perfectly*, *expertly* …*invisible.* …Sit, if you like. …It's a novelty, being *seen.* …I'll not pretend I don't …find it *strange.**

npcs.name: Garrick
npcs.title: who did the filthy work the world despised and depended on
npcs.hail: *(A big, stooped man sits apart, and he sits the way a person sits who has spent a life being told to keep his distance — hunched in on himself, taking up as little room as he can, his hands (huge, scarred, scrubbed raw) tucked away as if even he has been taught they are unclean. He does not look up at you at first, braced, the way the long-despised brace, for the curl of the lip, the step back, the held breath. For Garrick did the work, in life, that every city depends on utterly and cannot bear to look at: the gong-farmer's work, the nightsoil, the cesspits and the gutters, the carrying-away of all the filth that a city makes and a city would drown in within a week if no one carried it off — the most essential labor there is, and the most despised, done in the dark, by the ones the daylight world crossed the street to avoid.)* …You don't want to sit by me, friend. *(a low, rough voice, not unkind, just certain)* …I know how I — folk could always *smell* the trade on me, in life. *Years* off it now and I *still* sit downwind out of *habit*, still *flinch* when a clean soul comes near, waiting for the — *(he stops)* …Garrick. I was a *gong-farmer*, friend. The *nightsoil.* The *cesspits.* The work nobody — *(a bitter, tired breath)* …the work that *kept* the *whole* *city* from *drowning* in its *own* *filth*, and that *every* *soul* in that city would've *died* of *shame* to be *seen* *shaking* my *hand.* …Sit *upwind*, if you sit at all, friend. …I'm *used* to it. …I've been *upwind* of the *whole* *world* …my *whole* *life.**

npcs.name: Wennick
npcs.title: who tended the dying everyone else fled
npcs.hail: *(A quiet, gentle-handed soul sits near the table with the unhurried calm of one long accustomed to the presence of death, and there is about them a deep, worn tenderness — the particular gentleness of a person who has held a great many strangers' hands at the very end and learned to make that the most important work in the world. They look up at you with kind, unfrightened eyes; nothing about the dead, you sense, has frightened this one in a very long time.)* …Welcome, cold one. *(a soft, steady voice)* …Don't mind that I'm not startled by you — a crossed-over soul is no strange thing to me. I spent my life in rooms with the dying, and the dead were only ever the dying a little further along. *(a gentle smile)* …I'm Wennick. I tended the ones at the end — the plague-struck, the rotting, the mad, the ones whose own families had *fled* the *room*, who'd have died *alone* in the *dark* with *no* hand to hold if someone hadn't *come.* *(they fold their gentle hands)* …Someone always had to *come*, cold one. To do the thing no one *wanted* — to sit with the *contagious* and the *frightening* and the *foul*, to *wash* what needed washing, to *be* the *last* *face* a frightened soul *saw*, so that *no* one *died* *alone.* *(quietly)* …It was the most *unseen* work there is, cold one — *unseen* because people can't *bear* to *look* at it, the way they can't bear to look at *death* itself. …But *someone* *did* it. …I *did* it. …And I will tell you, if you'll sit a while …what I learned …in all those rooms …that the *whole* *world* …was too *afraid* …to *enter.**

variants.text: *(She straightens — a lifetime of correct posture — and regards you with her shrewd, tired eyes.)* …Sit, then. Mind the bench; I'd have had it mended in a day, in the house, but there's no one to *tell* down here, and that's the strangest thing about being *dead*, cold one — no one to *manage.* *(a dry breath)* …Fifty years I ran the Marlowe house. And I'll tell you what that *meant*, because folk never understood it, thought a housekeeper was a woman who *dusted.* *(she shakes her head)* …I ran a *staff* of *forty.* I kept the *accounts*, the *stores*, the *whole* *machine* of it — and the *machine*, cold one, was *enormous*, and it ran so *smooth* and so *silent* that the *family* *upstairs* genuinely *believed*, I think, that *bread* simply *appeared*, that *fires* *lit* themselves, that the *world* was *clean* and *warm* and *fed* by *nature.* *(quietly)* …That was the *art* of it. To make the *labor* *vanish.* So the *grand* lives upstairs could *float*, *free* and *light*, on a *foundation* of *work* they *never* *had* to *see.* *(she folds her ruined hands)* …And here is the *price* of doing that *art* *well*, cold one: …the *better* I did it, the *less* I *existed.* …A *perfect* *servant* …is an *invisible* one. …I gave *fifty* *years* …to becoming *very*, *very* *good* …at not *being* *there.* …*Ask* me what you like. …I find I've a *great* *deal* to *say* …for a woman …no one ever *listened* to.*

nodes.speaker: Tilda
nodes.text: *(She sits with her work-ruined hands folded, upright and attentive, the posture of a lifetime of waiting on others bent, for once, toward being waited *upon* — which she plainly finds peculiar.)* …Go on and ask. *(a wry look)* …It's an odd thing, cold one, to be the one *spoken* to instead of the one *spoken* *past.* Fifty years I stood in rooms while the great ones talked *over* me, *through* me, as if I were a *coat-stand* that *poured* the *tea.* …Being *asked* a *question* …I'll want a moment to get *used* to it.

choices.text: *(read the housekeeper — past the trained invisibility, to the vast hidden labor of a life)* "Let me really see you, Tilda. The thing fifty years of being invisible was hiding."
"Did it gall you, Tilda? Fifty years holding up grand lives that never saw you. Was there resentment under the good service?"

*(the one who knows the Wall credits the visible and misses the foundation — that the grand lives were built on her unseen hands)* "Tilda — here's the thing your whole invisible fifty years proved. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by what's *visible* — the grand deeds, the names that get remembered, the lives that *shine.* And it would look at you — a housekeeper, no monument, no name anyone knows, fifty years of work expertly made to *vanish* — and credit you nothing, the way the family upstairs credited you nothing, because your whole art was to not be *seen.* But you and I both know what actually happened in that house: the grand lives upstairs didn't *float.* They *stood* — on you. On forty pairs of hands you managed, on fires you laid, on a whole vast hidden machine of labor that you ran so well it disappeared. Take you out of that house, Tilda, and the grand lives don't dim a little — they *collapse*, within a week, into cold and hunger and filth, because they were never self-sustaining; they were *held up*, every hour, by the work no one looked at. The Wall credits the ones who *shine.* But a thing that shines is being *lit* — and someone, unseen, is tending the lamp. You weren't the absence of a great life, Tilda. You were the *foundation* a dozen great lives were built on, and a foundation doesn't get less essential for being underground. The world remembers the ones on top. It was *never* held up by them. It was held up by you."
"Tell me one thing you did, Tilda, that no one ever knew — one piece of the invisible work, so I'd know what holding a house up actually looks like."
"Rest a while, Tilda. I'll come back — and I'll still see you when I do."

nodes.speaker: Tilda
nodes.text: *(She permits your regard with a flicker of discomfort — being looked *at* is the one thing fifty years never taught her to bear — and you let your cold move past the trained invisibility to the vast life it concealed.)* *(And your sense finds a competence so total it takes your breath: not the narrow skill the world imagined, a woman who dusted, but the deep, broad, ceaseless mastery of a person who held an entire small world together in her head at once — forty souls and their quarrels and their wages, the stores and the seasons, the accounts to the farthing, the thousand small failures forestalled before anyone above stairs ever knew a failure was possible. Your sense reads that Tilda was, in the truest sense, a *governor* — that she ran a community of forty with more skill than the master of the house ran his estate, and with none of the credit, because the master's work was *visible* and hers was designed to vanish. And your sense reads the deeper thing, the thing under the competence: that the labor was not cold. That Tilda did not merely *manage* her forty; she *tended* them — knew which scullery-maid was grieving and gave her the lighter work that week, which footman was sending wages to a sick mother and quietly saw them sent, held the whole below-stairs world together not just as a machine but as a *family* she mothered without ever being thanked for the mothering. And your sense reads the cost, the thing she carries: that fifty years of being *expertly invisible* did not leave her untouched, that a soul needs, somewhere, to be *seen*, and that Tilda's was not — that she poured her whole formidable self into holding up lives that genuinely did not know she existed, and went to her grave having been, to the people whose entire world she sustained, less real than the furniture. And your sense reads the question that has sat in her across an age, the one she is too proud to ask: not *did my work matter* — she knows it did — but *did I matter*, the woman, apart from the work; was there ever a self there to be valued, or only a perfect function; and whether a life spent so completely in service to others' lives was a life that was ever, for one hour, her own. She does not need to be told she was a good housekeeper. She needs to be told she was a *someone* — that the foundation has a face, and the face was always worth seeing, even when no one looked.)*

nodes.speaker: Tilda
nodes.text: *(She considers the question with the honesty of a soul past all need to perform contentment.)* …Did it *gall* me. *(a dry breath)* …You want the *true* answer, cold one, not the *servant's* answer — the servant's answer is *'no, I was* glad *to* serve,' and you say it so *long* you half *believe* it. *(quietly)* …The *true* answer is: not the *work.* The *work* I was *proud* of — *fierce* proud, the way a *craftsman* is, and *no* one can *take* that from me, I ran that house like a *ship* and I *knew* it even if *they* didn't. *(her jaw sets)* …What *galled* me, cold one — what *still* does, a little — was the — *(she hunts for it)* …the *theft* of it. Not the *wages*; the wages were the wages. The theft of the *crediting.* *(her voice hardens)* …The young master would stand in a *warm* *clean* *well-run* house and say to a *guest*, *proud*, *'we* keep a *good* *house'* — *we* — as though *he* had *laid* a *single* *fire* in his *life*, as though the *warmth* and the *order* he was *taking* *credit* for hadn't been *built*, *brick* by *invisible* *brick*, by *me* and my *forty*, while he *slept.* *(barely)* …*That's* the gall, cold one. Not being *unpaid.* Being *un-credited* — watching the ones *upstairs* take the *praise* for a *thing* *I* *made*, *because* the *making* of it was *beneath* their *notice.* *(she folds her hands)* …But I'll tell you the thing that *kept* me from going *sour* on it, cold one. *(quietly)* …I *knew.* …*I* knew whose hands held that house up. …*My* *forty* knew. …And in the end, cold one … …*knowing* you did the thing …*matters* more …than *anyone* *seeing* you do it. …It has to. …For the *unseen* … …*knowing* …is the *only* *crediting* …we *ever* *get.**

nodes.speaker: Tilda
nodes.text: *(A faint, fierce pride lights her tired face — she is, you realize, delighted to be asked for the specifics of a craft no one ever wanted to hear about.)* …One thing. *(she almost smiles)* …Cold one, there were *ten thousand* — but I'll give you the *kind* of thing, the *invisible* *kind.* …The *winter* the *fever* came through. *Bad* one. Half the *staff* down, and the family *upstairs* with a *wedding* *planned*, *two* *hundred* *guests*, the *whole* *county* coming. *(she leans in)* …If that wedding had *failed* — cold larders, no fires, a half-staffed house shamed before the county — it would have been a *catastrophe*, the *family's* *name* *dragged* through every *drawing* *room* for a *decade.* *(her eyes gleam)* …And it *didn't* *fail*, cold one. It went off *flawless.* *Glorious.* They *talk* about that wedding *still*, I'd *wager*, the *Marlowe* *wedding*, how *perfect* it *was.* *(quietly)* …And *not* *one* *soul* at that wedding — not the *family*, not the *guests*, not the *bride* — *ever* *knew* that it went *flawless* because the *housekeeper* nursed *half* her *staff* through a *fever* with *one* *hand* while she *re-planned* the *entire* *event* around the *gaps* with the *other*, didn't *sleep* for *six* *days*, *called* in *favors* from *every* house in the *county*, and *held* the *whole* *thing* *together* by *will* *alone.* *(barely)* …They *remember* a *perfect* *wedding*, cold one. …They have *no* *idea* it was a *near*-*catastrophe* that *one* *exhausted* *woman* *below* *stairs* *turned* into a *triumph* …*precisely* so they'd *never* *have* to *know* there'd been *danger* at *all.* *(she meets your eyes)* …*That's* the *work*, cold one. …The *better* you do it … …the *less* anyone *knows* it was *ever* *done.* …I *saved* that *family's* *name.* …And the *price* of *saving* it *so* *well* … …is that they'll *never* *know* …it *needed* *saving.**

nodes.speaker: Tilda
nodes.text: *(She listens, upright and still, and slowly her work-ruined hands tighten in her lap, and when you finish the shrewd tired eyes are bright, and she presses her lips together hard, the way a proud soul does when it will not weep in front of company.)* …*'The world was never held up by the ones on top. It was held up by you.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …Fifty years. …And *no* one — not *once* — *said* it. *(her voice is tight, controlled, and breaking under the control)* …They *thanked* me, *sometimes*, the *kinder* ones — *'thank* you, *Tilda'* — but it was the *thanks* you give a *good* *tool*, cold one, *'the* *axe* *cut* *well.'* …*No* one *ever* *said* the *thing* you've *said*: that the *grand* *lives* didn't *float* — that they *stood*, on *me* — that *take* *me* out and they *fall.* *(she presses a ruined hand to her chest)* …I *knew* it. I *told* you I *knew* it. …But there is a *difference*, cold one, between *knowing* a thing *alone* in the *dark* of your *own* *heart* …and having *one* *soul*, *once*, *say* it *back* to you *out* *loud.* *(and the control breaks, and she lets it, with dignity)* …And your *Wall.* *(fierce now, through it)* …Your Wall is the *family* *upstairs*, *cold* one, *grown* *vast.* It *credits* the ones who *shine* and it *cannot* *see* the *hands* that *hold* the *shining* *up* — it thinks the *grand* *souls* *float*, *self-made*, *deserving*, and it *mortars* the *foundation* *out* of *sight* *beneath* them and *calls* the *building* *theirs.* *(she stands, upright, magnificent in her plainness)* …*Go* and *tell* it, cold one, what a *housekeeper* *knows*: …*nothing* *shines* *by* *itself.* …*Every* *grand* *thing* that *ever* *floated* …was *held* *up*, *unseen*, by a *hundred* *hands* the *Wall* *won't* *count.* …And the *foundation*, cold one — *(her voice rings)* …the *foundation* …is *worth* *more* …than the *whole* *house* it *carries.* …Because the *house* *can* *fall* …and a *new* one *rise* …but *without* the *foundation* …there is *no* *house* …*at* *all.* *(barely, fierce)* …*Go* *credit* the *uncredited*, cold one. …*Tell* the *unseen* …their *hands* …*held* …the *world.**

nodes.speaker: Tilda
nodes.text: *(She nods, composing herself, and the upright dignity reasserts — but warmer now, as if a window has been opened in a long-shut room.)* …Go on, cold one. *(a truer smile)* …And see the *other* two, while you're below-stairs — they're *my* *kind*, the *unseen*, though the world *ranked* us *differently* even *down* *here*, which is a *bitter* little *joke.* *(she nods toward Garrick)* …*Garrick*, there. *(her face softens, and something fierce comes into it)* …Don't you *dare* sit *upwind* of him, cold one, the way he'll *tell* you to. …That man did the *filthiest* and the *most* *necessary* work there *is* — the *nightsoil*, the *cesspits* — and the world *taught* him, *cruelly*, *thoroughly*, that *he* was *filthy*, when the *truth* is the *city* would have *drowned* in a *week* without him. *He's* the *worst*-*wounded* of us *three*, cold one. *Go* and sit *close.* …And *Wennick*, by the table — *(her voice goes gentle, reverent)* …*Wennick* tended the *dying* that *everyone* *fled.* …If *I* held a *house* up and *Garrick* held a *city* up … …*Wennick* held up the *one* thing, cold one, that *no* one *else* would *touch* *at* *all*: …the *frightened*, the *foul*, the *dying-alone.* …*Go* to *all* *three* of us, cold one. …We're the *floor* …the *whole* *world* *stood* on …*and* *never* *looked* *down* *to* *see.**

variants.text: *(He shifts, still angled away from you, still keeping that careful downwind distance, his huge scrubbed-raw hands tucked out of sight.)* …You stayed. *(a low, wary rumble)* …And you sat — *(he glances, surprised)* …you sat *near.* Most don't. Most find a reason to be *elsewhere* once they hear the trade. *(he doesn't quite believe it)* …I'll tell you plain, friend, what I was, so you can move off if you've a mind. I emptied the *cesspits.* Carried the *nightsoil.* Cleaned the *gutters* and the *gong-holes* and all the *filth* a city makes and *can't* *abide* the *sight* of. *(quietly)* …Did it at *night*, friend, because the *daylight* world couldn't *bear* to *watch.* Lived *apart*, married *late* and *low*, was *crossed*-*away*-*from* in the *street* my *whole* *life* — folk holding their *noses*, their *children*, their *breath* — *(a bitter, worn breath)* …and the *thing* of it, friend, the thing that *got* *in* and *stayed*, is that after a *lifetime* of being treated like the *filth* I *carried* … …you start to *believe* it. …That *you're* the *dirty* thing. …Not the *work* — *you.* …I *scrubbed* these *hands* *raw* every *night* of my *life*, friend … …and I *never* *once* …felt *clean.**

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(He stays half-turned away, braced, but he does not leave, and there is in the set of his huge shoulders a wary, fragile readiness to be spoken to like a person — a thing he plainly does not expect and cannot quite trust.)* …Ask, if you want. *(low)* …I'll warn you, friend, I'm not — I never learned the *talking* much. Folk didn't *talk* to me, mostly, so I never got the *practice.* …But I'll try. …It's — *(he stops)* …it's not *nothing*, friend, being *asked.* …It's not *nothing* *at* *all.*

choices.text: *(read the gong-farmer — past the world's contempt and his own, to the truth of what he carried)* "Let me look at you, Garrick. Past what they told you you were. At what you actually did."
"Tell me about the work, Garrick. Truly. What the city would have been without the men who carried its filth away."

*(lift the thing the world put in him — that HE was the filth — without holding your nose while you do it)* "Garrick — look at me, and hear this from a soul sitting close on purpose, downwind of nothing. The world did a cruel thing to you, the cruelest thing it does to the ones it needs and despises: it taught you that because you handled what was dirty, *you* were dirty. That's a lie, and it's the exact lie that let everyone else stay comfortable. The filth you carried wasn't *yours* — it was *theirs.* Every soul in that city made it, every day, and could not have survived a week with it piling up — and you were the one who took *their* filth away so *they* could stay clean, and they paid you for it by deciding *you* were the unclean thing, so they'd never have to feel the shame of needing you. You were never the dirty one, Garrick. You were the cleanest service there is — the one who took the worst of everyone else's making and carried it off into the dark so the world could wake up clean. The hands you scrubbed raw weren't washing off *your* filth. They were washing off a whole city's, every night, and the city repaid you by making you carry its *shame* too, on top of its waste. Set it down. It was never yours. You handled the dirt. You were never it."

*(the one who knows the Wall despises what it cannot bear to need)* "Garrick — the grey Wall I'm walking toward does to souls exactly what the city did to you. It depends, utterly, on things it refuses to honor — the unclaimed dead it feeds on, the small souls it grinds up to keep its order running — and it can't *bear* to admit it needs them, so it calls them *worthless*, *low*, *deserving* of the mortar, the way the city called you filth so it wouldn't have to thank the man who saved it from drowning in its own waste. That's the oldest trick of every cruel order, Garrick: despise the thing you can't survive without, so you never have to feel grateful to it. You weren't despised because you were low. You were despised because you were *needed* — because every clean soul who crossed the street from you knew, somewhere, that they'd have died in their own filth without you, and couldn't bear the shame of the debt. The Wall does the same to every soul it mortars. And the proof that the contempt is a lie is simple: take away the despised, and the whole proud clean order *collapses.* You were never beneath the city, Garrick. You were holding it up from underneath, where it refused to look — and it spat on you precisely *because* it could not afford to lose you."
"Sit easy, Garrick. I'll come back — and I'll sit downwind of nothing."

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(He flinches as your attention settles on him — being *looked* at is a thing the despised learn to dread — but he holds still, and lets you, and you let your cold move past the cringe and the contempt-turned-inward to the truth of the man.)* *(And your sense finds, first, that he is clean. Not ironically, not metaphorically — your sense reads a soul scrubbed, by a lifetime of raw-handed effort, cleaner than almost any you have touched, because Garrick spent his whole life so terrified of the filth they said he was that he washed, and washed, in body and in conduct both: never cheated, never cruel, never took more than his due, raised his late low children gentle and honest, was kinder to the few who were kind to him than they ever deserved — a meticulously, painfully *good* man, made good in part by the desperate need to prove, to himself if no one else, that he was not the dirty thing the world named him. And your sense reads the cruelty of his trade with new eyes: that the work itself was not degrading — it was *sacred*, in the oldest sense, the necessary handling of the unclean that every civilization has always needed and always despised, the labor without which the bright cities simply die — and that the degradation was never in the work but in the *lie* laid over it, the story the clean world told to avoid its own debt: *if we call him filth, we need not thank him; if he is low by nature, we owe him nothing.* And your sense reads how thoroughly that lie got *inside* him, how a lifetime of being crossed-away-from in the street wore a groove so deep that Garrick came to believe it himself, to sit downwind of clean souls from reflex, to tuck his scrubbed hands away as if they were the offense — and reads the terrible loneliness of it, a man who did one of the most essential jobs in all the world and was made to feel, every day, that his very existence was an imposition on the people whose lives he saved. And your sense reads the thing that could free him, if he could hear it: that the filth was never his. That he was the cleanest kind of servant there is — the one who takes away what everyone makes and no one will own — and that the shame he carried his whole life was not his shame but the *city's*, ladled onto him because someone had to carry that too, the unbearable knowledge of how much the proud clean world depends on the despised dark work it cannot look at. He does not need to be told the work mattered. He half-knows that. He needs to be told that *he* was never the dirty thing — that he handled the filth and was, himself, all along, clean.)*

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(Something shifts in him — a craftsman's pride, long buried under shame, stirring at being asked about the actual work.)* …The work. *(a low breath)* …You *really* want to *know*, friend? Nobody *ever* — they want it *gone*, the filth, they *never* want to know *how.* *(he sits up, a little)* …All right. Here's the *truth* of it. A city, friend — any city, the *grandest* one you can name, with its *white* *towers* and its *clean* *squares* and its *proud* *folk* in their *fine* *clothes* — a city makes *filth.* *Tons* of it. *Every* *day.* It comes out of *every* *body* in it, *high* and *low*, *king* and *beggar*, and it does *not* *stop*, and it does *not* *care* how *fine* your *clothes* are. *(his voice gains strength)* …And if *no* one *carries* it *away*, friend — if the *gong*-*farmers* *stop*, even for a *week* — that *grand* *clean* city *drowns.* In *days.* The *filth* *rises*, the *wells* go *foul*, the *fever* comes, and the *white* *towers* *empty* into *plague*-*pits.* *(he meets your eyes, fierce now)* …I have *seen* what happens, friend, when the *carrying*-*away* *fails.* …I have *carried* *out* the *dead* of a *fever* that came *because* a *district* went *too* *long* *uncleaned.* …The *whole* of a *city's* *life*, friend — its *trade*, its *art*, its *kings*, its *poetry*, *all* of it — *sits* on *top* of *one* *brute* *fact*: that *someone* *carries* the *filth* *away* in the *dark* …so the *rest* can *pretend* it *isn't* *made.* *(quietly, the pride and the bitterness together)* …I was that *someone*, friend. …*Me* and my *kind.* …We *held* the *whole* *bright* *city* up …*from* the *bottom* of the *cesspit* …and it *thanked* us …by *holding* its *nose.**

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(You sit close — you make a point of it — and you don't praise the work while holding your breath, the way the rare kind ones always did; you tell him, plainly, looking right at him, that the filth was never *his*, that he carried a whole city's shame on top of its waste, that he was the clean one all along. And a man who scrubbed his hands raw every night for a lifetime and never once felt clean hears, at last, that he always was.)* *(His huge scarred hands come slowly out from where he's tucked them away, and he looks at them — really looks — and his weathered face crumples.)* …never mine. *(barely)* …the filth was — it was *theirs.* All of it. I just — I *carried* it, and they — they made me *carry* the *shame* of it *too*, so *they* wouldn't have to *feel* it. *(he turns his raw scrubbed hands over)* …friend, I scrubbed these — *every* *night* — *fifty* *years* — and I never *understood* why I never felt *clean*, no matter *how* *hard* — and it's because I was scrubbing at the *wrong* *thing.* …The *dirt* came *off* *easy.* …It was the *shame* that *wouldn't* *wash* — and the *shame* was never *mine* to *wash* *off*, it was *theirs*, *ladled* *onto* me, and *no* amount of *scrubbing* *my* *hands* could *ever* — *(he presses the heels of those huge hands to his eyes)* …I was *clean*, friend. …The *whole* *time.* …I handled the *dirt* …and I was never *it.* *(and the big stooped man slowly, for the first time since you came, sits *up*, uncurls, lets himself take up the room he's always made himself small in)* …fifty years downwind of the world, friend. …and you sat *close*, and *told* me *true*, and I — *(a wet, disbelieving breath)* …I think I can *stop* *scrubbing* now. …It was never going to *work* anyway. …Because there was never anything *on* me …*to* *get* *off.* …*thank* you, friend. …you didn't hold your *nose.* …in my *whole* *life* and *death* …you're the *first* …who *didn't* …hold …their *nose.**

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(You tell him the filth was never his, and you sit close while you say it, and something in the big stooped man eases — slowly, warily, an old groove not undone in a moment.)* …never mine. *(slowly)* …you say it like it's — like it's *simple*, friend. *'You* handled the *dirt*, you weren't *it.'* *(he turns his raw hands over)* …and *part* of me — the part that *always* *knew*, deep down, that the *work* was *honest* — that part *hears* you. *(quietly)* …but fifty years, friend, of folk holding their *noses*, crossing the *street* — that *gets* *in*, *deep*, deeper than *knowing*, and it doesn't come *out* because *one* *kind* *soul* *says* the *right* *thing*, however *true.* *(he meets your eyes, and there's the first crack of hope)* …but you *sat* *close.* You didn't *flinch.* …That's — friend, you've *no* *idea* what *that* is, after a *life* of *flinching.* *(a rough, unsteady breath)* …Come *back.* …Sit *close* *again.* …Say it *again*, the *'never* *yours'* part. …I think if a *clean* soul *keeps* *sitting* *close* …and *keeps* *saying* it … …the *groove* might *wear* *shallow* *enough*, *someday*, …that I could *believe* it. …I *want* to *believe* it, friend. …I've *wanted* to *feel* *clean* …my *whole* *life.**

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(You mean to lift it — but you praise the work a little too brightly, *what you did was so important, someone had to do it* — and the big man hears, under the praise, the faint old note that always accompanied it, and his shoulders curl back in.)* …*'Someone* had to do it.' *(a low, tired breath)* …Aye, friend. That's — that's the *kind* one's words, that is. *'Someone* had to do the *dirty* work, and *thank* *goodness* for *folk* like *you.'* *(he shakes his head, not angry, just worn)* …And it's *meant* *warm*, I *know* it is. But you *hear* it, friend, the thing *under* it? *'Someone* had to' — meaning a *someone* like *me*, a *low* someone, a someone it's *all* *right* to *spend* on the *filth* because — *(he tucks his hands away again)* …it still puts me *down* there, friend. *'Thank* *goodness* *someone* low *enough* would *do* it.' …It *thanks* the *work* …and it *leaves* *me* …in the *cesspit.* *(quietly)* …Come back, friend, when you can say a thing that doesn't — that doesn't *thank* me for being *low* *enough* to *do* it. …Say a thing that pulls me *out* of the *pit* …*not* one that *pats* me on the *head* *for* *being* *willing* to *stay* *in* it. …There's a *difference*, friend. …It's the *whole* *difference* …I *spent* my *life* …on the *wrong* *side* of.*

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong in the way that wounds deepest — you reach to reassure him and, without meaning to, you do the small involuntary thing, the thing everyone did: as you speak of the filth, some flicker crosses your face, the faintest recoil at the *idea* of it — and Garrick, who has read that exact flicker on ten thousand faces, sees it, and goes very still, and curls away.)* …there it is. *(barely, flat)* …you didn't *mean* to, friend. They *never* *mean* to. …But I *saw* it. The little — the *flinch.* The *face* doing the thing, when you *thought* about the *cesspit*, the *what*-*I*-*carried.* *(he tucks his hands all the way out of sight)* …you can *say* the *kind* *words*, friend — *'never* yours, you were *clean'* — but your *face* said the *true* thing, the thing *under* the words: that I'm — that the *idea* of *me* makes a *clean* soul *recoil*, just a *little*, *can't* *help* it. *(quietly, and it is worse than anger)* …and that's — friend, that's the thing I *can't* *get* *past*, the thing that *taught* me I was *filth* in the *first* *place*: not the *words*, the words were *sometimes* *kind* — …the *flinch.* The *involuntary* *flinch.* The *body* *telling* the *truth* the *mouth* was *too* *polite* to *say.* *(he turns away)* …*go*, friend. …I *know* you *meant* *well.* …They *all* *meant* *well.* …But you *can't* *lift* a man *out* of the *pit* …while your *face* is still *recoiling* …from the *smell* of it. …Come *back* …when you can *think* of the *cesspit* …and *not* *flinch.* …*Then* — *(barely)* …*then* I might *believe* …you *see* a *man* …and not …a *trade.**

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the words land somewhere a lifetime of contempt had sealed off, and when you finish he is quiet a long moment, and then the big stooped man lifts his head, and there is something in his weathered face that has never been there before: the beginning of being *upright.*)* …*'Despised because you were needed.'* *(barely)* …friend. …that — *(he turns it over, slow, the way a careful man tests a thing he can't quite believe)* …that *fits.* That *fits* a thing I *felt* my *whole* *life* and could *never* *name*: that the *worse* they *treated* me, the *more* they — it was almost like the *contempt* got *stronger* the *more* they *needed* me, and I never *understood* it, I thought *'if* I'm so *useful*, why do they *hate* me *more*, not *less' —* *(his voice gains an edge of hard, dawning clarity)* …and you've *named* it. *They hated me* because *they needed me.* …Because *every* *clean* soul that *crossed* the *street* *knew*, *deep* *down*, they'd *drown* in their *own* *filth* without me — and *couldn't* *bear* the *debt* — so they *made* me *low*, *made* me *filth*, so they'd *never* have to *say* *thank* *you* to the *man* who *saved* them. *(he flexes his huge scarred hands, and does not, this time, tuck them away)* …and your *Wall*, friend. *(low, fierce)* …it's the *same.* It *feeds* on the *souls* it *calls* *worthless* — *needs* them, *runs* on them — and *calls* them *low* *precisely* *so* it never has to *admit* the *debt.* *(he sits up, all the way, uncurling, big and clean and finally unbowed)* …*go* and *tell* it, friend, what a *gong*-*farmer* *knows*: …*the thing a cruel order despises most …is the thing it cannot survive without.* …*Look* at what your *Wall* *spits* on, friend. …*That's* what's *holding* it *up.* …The *contempt* is just the *Wall* …*too* *proud* …to say *thank* *you* …to the *hands* …in the *dark* …that *carry* its *filth* *away.* *(barely, fierce)* …*Honor* the *despised*, friend. …*They're* the *foundation.* …*They* *always* *were.* …*Go.* …And — *(he almost smiles)* …*tell* old *Tilda* I *sat* *up* *straight.* …She's been *at* me about it …an *age.**

nodes.speaker: Garrick
nodes.text: *(He gives you a wary, grateful nod, and does not, you notice, edge any further downwind as you rise.)* …Go on, friend. *(low)* …And — thank you. For sitting *close.* …It's not *nothing.* I *keep* saying that, and it *keeps* being *true.* *(he nods toward the others)* …See *Tilda* again, and *Wennick.* …We're a *set*, the *three* of us, friend — the *unseen* — though even *down* *here*, the *world's* *ranking* *clings*: *Tilda* kept a *grand* house, so she's *almost* *respectable*; *me*, I did the *filth*, so I'm the *low* one of the *low.* *(a tired, knowing breath)* …but you should hear *Wennick*, friend, before you go. *(his rough voice gentles)* …*Wennick* tended the *dying.* The *plague*-*struck*, the *rotting*, the ones whose *own* *families* *ran.* …And here's the thing, friend, the thing that *humbles* even *me*: …*I* carried away the filth …but I did it at *night*, *apart*, *away* from the *folk* who *made* it. …*Wennick* sat *right* *down* *in* the *worst* of it — *held* the *hands* of the *contagious* and the *foul* — did the *one* unseen work …that *can't* be done at a *distance.* *(quietly)* …*Go* to *Wennick*, friend. …Of the *three* of us … …*Wennick's* is the *bravest* *kind* of *unseen.* …The kind …you have to *touch.**

variants.text: *(They settle their gentle hands in their lap and regard you with the deep, unfrightened calm of a soul that has sat with the worst the body can do and learned to stay.)* …Sit, cold one. *(the soft, steady voice)* …I'll tell you what I did, plainly, because the others will have sent you and there's no use being coy. …I sat with the dying. *(a gentle nod)* …Not the *easy* dying — the *peaceful* old soul in a *clean* bed, surrounded by *loving* family; *those* never *wanted* for *company.* …The *other* kind, cold one. The *plague*-*struck*, whose *touch* could *kill* you. The *rotting*, whose *rooms* you could not *enter* without *gagging.* The *mad*, who did not *know* you. The *poor*, who had *no* *one.* …The ones, cold one, whose *own* *families* had *fled* the *room* — *understandably*, *humanly*, out of *terror* or *grief* or *the* *plain* *animal* *fear* of *catching* it — leaving a *frightened* soul to die *alone* in the *dark.* *(they fold their gentle hands)* …And *someone*, cold one, *has* to *come.* …No one should *die* *alone* and *afraid* in the *dark* …merely because their *dying* was *frightening* or *foul* or *catching.* …*Someone* has to *be* the *last* *kind* *face.* …*Someone* has to *come* *in* …when *everyone* *else* has *gone* *out.* …*Ask* me what you will, cold one. …I have sat at *more* *ends* …than *almost* *any* *soul* *living* … …and I *learned* things there …in the *rooms* the *world* *fled* …that the *world* …will *never* *know.**

nodes.speaker: Wennick
nodes.text: *(They wait with the bottomless patience of a soul that has sat through ten thousand long last nights and learned that there is nowhere it needs to be but here, with you, now.)* …Ask, cold one. *(a soft smile)* …Take your time. I learned, in the dying-rooms, that *hurry* is the one thing the frightened cannot bear — that the greatest gift you can give a soul is the sense that you are in no rush to be anywhere but beside them. …So ask slow, if you like. I am not going anywhere.

choices.text: *(read the one who tended the dying — past the calm, to what it took to keep entering the rooms the world fled)* "Let me look at you, Wennick. At what it cost to keep walking in when everyone else walked out."
"What did you learn, Wennick? In the rooms the world fled — at all those ends. Tell me the thing the world will never know."

*(the one who knows the Wall flees the unbearable — and that the soul who walks toward it is the bravest, most needed thing there is)* "Wennick — you're the deepest version of the thing I've found below-stairs, and the one the grey Wall fails most completely. The Wall flees what it can't bear — it sorts away the frightening, the foul, the inconvenient dead, mortars them out of sight so the comfortable order never has to look at them, exactly the way those families fled the dying-rooms. It's a machine for *not having to be present* to suffering it finds unbearable. And you are the living refutation of it — a soul whose entire life was *walking toward* the unbearable, into the rooms everyone else fled, to make sure that no one, however frightening or foul or contagious their dying, faced the end *alone.* That's not a small kindness, Wennick. It's the largest one there is, because it's the one that costs the most and earns the least and can't be done at a distance — you had to *be there*, in the room, in the dark, holding the hand that might kill you. The world honors the souls who achieve, who build, who shine. It has no monument for the ones who simply *stayed* — who walked into the worst rooms and held the worst hands so a stranger wouldn't die afraid and alone. And that staying, Wennick, is the exact opposite of the Wall: the Wall abandons; you accompanied. Bring down a Wall and you've stopped a cruelty. But *be* what you were — the one who comes in when everyone goes out — and you've done the kinder, harder thing the Wall could never even conceive of."
"Weren't you afraid, Wennick? Of catching it — the plague, the rot, the death in those rooms? How does a soul keep walking toward the thing everyone else runs from?"
"Rest a while, Wennick. I'll come back — and I won't be in any hurry when I do."

nodes.speaker: Wennick
nodes.text: *(They let you look, calm and open, and you let your cold move into the deep worn tenderness to find what sustained it.)* *(And your sense finds, first, that the calm is not detachment. You half-expect it — that a soul could only do this work by going numb, by holding the dying at arm's length, by not letting it in — and your sense finds the opposite, finds that Wennick let *all* of it in, every single time, felt the full terror and grief and foulness of each death and did not flinch from feeling it, because they understood, with a wisdom that staggers you, that the whole *point* was to be *present* — that a numb companion is no companion, that the dying can tell when you have left even while your body remains, and that the one thing Wennick had to give, the only thing, was the total, undefended, fully-feeling *presence* of another human being who was not going to look away. And your sense reads the cost of that — that Wennick grieved every one, that there was no armor, that each of the ten thousand ends went all the way in and left its mark, and that Wennick carried the accumulated weight of more witnessed suffering than any soul should and did not let it close them, because closing would have meant the next frightened soul died with a shell beside them instead of a person. And your sense reads *why* they could bear it, the thing that kept them walking into the rooms: not duty, not piety, but a single unshakable conviction, learned at the first deathbed and confirmed at every one after — that the worst thing in all the world is not death, not pain, not even the foulest dying, but to face any of those *alone*; that a held hand does not stop death but transforms it entirely, turns a terror into a passage, a solitary horror into a shared and human thing; and that therefore the simple act of *staying* — of being the one who comes in when all others go out — is not a minor mercy but very nearly the most important work a soul can do, because it addresses the deepest fear there is, the fear of dying alone and unwitnessed in the dark. And your sense reads the thing Wennick has never been told and does not ask for: that they were right, that the staying mattered more than they will ever know, that ten thousand frightened souls crossed over with a kind face as the last thing they saw *because Wennick chose to walk into the room* — and that this, the accompanying of the dying, the refusing to let anyone face the end alone, is a kind of greatness the world has no word for because the world cannot bear to look long enough at death to see who was sitting beside it.)*

nodes.speaker: Wennick
nodes.text: *(They are quiet a moment, gathering the deepest thing they know, and when they speak the soft voice carries the authority of ten thousand last nights.)* …What I learned. *(softly)* …Cold one, I learned the thing the *whole* *world* spends its *whole* *life* *fleeing* the *rooms* to *avoid* learning — and it is a *gentler* thing than the *world* *fears*, which is the *saddest* part, that the world *runs* from a truth that would *comfort* it. *(they fold their gentle hands)* …I learned that at the *very* *end*, cold one — at the *actual* *threshold*, when the *body* is *failing* and the *self* is *loosening* and there is *no* more *time* for *pretense* — *every* soul, *every* *single* one, *high* or *low*, *saint* or *wretch*, wants the *same* *three* *things.* …*Not* to be *alone.* …To be *forgiven* — for *something*, *everyone* has a *something.* …And to *know* that their *life* *mattered* …that they were *real* …that they will be *remembered*, even a *little*, even by *one.* *(barely)* …*That's* it, cold one. *That's* the *whole* of what a *soul* *needs* at the *end.* …*Not* *wealth.* *Not* *triumph.* *Not* the *grand* *things* they *spent* their *lives* *chasing.* …Just: *don't let me be alone, tell me I'm forgiven, and tell me I mattered.* *(they meet your eyes)* …And I could *give* all *three*, cold one. …I could *stay*, so they weren't *alone.* …I could *say* *'you're* forgiven,' and *mean* it, and a *dying* soul does *not* *quibble* over *whether* you have the *authority* — *kindness* *is* the *authority.* …And I could *say* *'you* *mattered*, *I* will *remember* you' — and I *did*, cold one, I *remember* *every* one. *(softly)* …*That's* what I learned in the *rooms* the world *fled*: …that the *whole* of a *human* *life*, *all* its *fear* and *striving*, comes *down*, at the *end*, to *three* *small* *things* …that *cost* *nothing* to *give* …and that the *world* *flees* the *room* …*rather* than *give.* …I gave them, cold one. …*Ten* *thousand* *times.* …*That* …was my *whole* *unseen* *life.**

nodes.speaker: Wennick
nodes.text: *(They consider the question with their unhurried honesty.)* …Was I afraid. *(softly)* …Of *course*, cold one. I was not a *fool*, nor a *saint* — I knew *exactly* what a *plague*-*room* could *give* me, and it *gave* it, in the *end*; I *died* of a *fever* I *caught* at a *bedside*, tending a soul whose *own* *kin* had *fled.* *(a gentle, unregretful smile)* …So *yes.* I was *afraid*, *every* *room*, *every* *time.* The *fear* never *left.* *(they fold their hands)* …But here is the thing I *understood*, cold one, that let me *keep* *walking* *in*: …I weighed the *two* *fears* against each *other.* …*My* fear — of *catching* it, of *dying* — was *real*, and *large.* …But the *fear* on the *bed*, cold one — the fear of the *soul* *facing* the *end* *alone* in the *dark*, *abandoned*, with *no* hand and *no* face and *no* one — *(barely)* …*that* fear was *larger.* …And it was *happening* *right* *now*, to a *real* *soul*, *in* *that* *room*, whether I *entered* or *not.* *(they meet your eyes)* …So it was *never*, *really*, a choice between *safe* and *afraid*, cold one. …It was a *choice* between *my* fear …and *theirs.* …Between *me* *risking* the *catching* …and *them* *dying* *alone.* *(softly)* …And *every* *time*, cold one — *every* single time, with my *own* fear *screaming* — I found that I *could* *not* *purchase* my *own* *safety* …with *their* *solitude.* …I could *not* *buy* my *un*-*afraid* …with *their* *dying*-*alone.* …The *price* was *too* *high*, cold one. …Not for *them.* …For *me.* …I'd *rather* *catch* the *fever* …than *live* *having* *let* a *frightened* soul …die in the *dark* …because I was *too* *afraid* …to *hold* …its *hand.* …*(quietly)* …And in the end I *did* *catch* it. …And I have *never*, *once* …*regretted* …the *bedside* …where I *caught* it.*

nodes.speaker: Wennick
nodes.text: *(They listen, and the deep calm does not break, but it *deepens*, and their gentle eyes shine, and when you finish they bow their head, the way one bows to a truth long carried and finally named by another.)* …*'The Wall abandons. You accompanied.'* *(softly)* …Cold one. …I never had the *words* for the *shape* of my *life* until *now.* I knew the *work* — the *staying*, the *coming-in-when-others-go-out* — but I never had the *frame*, the *why* it was the *thing* I was *for.* *(they press their gentle hands together)* …And you've given it: that the *deepest* *cruelty* is *abandonment* — the *leaving-alone* of a soul in its *worst* hour — and that the *deepest* *kindness*, its *exact* *opposite*, is *presence*: the *simple*, *costly*, *undefended* *staying*, the *being-there* when *being-there* is *hard* and *frightening* and *earns* you *nothing.* *(their voice fills)* …And your *Wall*, cold one. *(quietly, with a profound certainty)* …*Yes.* It is a *machine* for *abandonment.* …It *sorts* the *dead* into the *kept* and the *cast-out*, and it *casts* *out* the *frightening*, the *foul*, the *inconvenient*, the *owed-nothing* — *mortars* them *away* so the *comfortable* *order* *never* has to *be* *present* to them — *exactly* as those *families* *fled* the *dying*-*rooms.* …The *Wall* is the *whole* *world*, cold one, *fleeing* the *room* it *cannot* *bear* to *sit* in. *(they look at you, and there is a gentle fire in it)* …So I will tell you the thing I *learned* at *ten* *thousand* *bedsides*, to *carry* to your *Wall*: …you do *not* *defeat* *abandonment* …by *force.* …You *defeat* it …by *walking* *in.* …By being the *one* who *comes* *in* …when *all* *the* *world* has *gone* *out.* …*Go* to your *Wall*, cold one — to the *mortared*, the *cast-out*, the *abandoned* — …and do the *one* thing the *Wall* *cannot* *conceive* of: …*sit* *down* *beside* them. …*Stay.* …*Don't* *flee* the *room.* …*Tell* each *abandoned* *soul* the *three* *things* every *dying* *soul* *needs*: …*you are not alone. you are forgiven. you mattered, and I will remember you.* *(barely)* …A *Wall*, cold one … …is *only* the *world's* *fear* …of *sitting* *with* the *unbearable.* …*Be* the *one* …who *sits.* …*That* …*unmakes* it.*

nodes.speaker: Wennick
nodes.text: *(They incline their head, unhurried, and the deep calm of them seems to make the whole below-stairs a little quieter, a little safer.)* …Go gently, cold one. *(a soft smile)* …And — you've met all three of us now, the *unseen.* *Tilda*, who held a *house* up. *Garrick*, who held a *city* up. And *me.* *(quietly)* …We three are the *answer*, cold one, to the *whole* *grand* *lie* of the *world* — and of your *Wall.* The lie that the *world* is *held* up by the ones *on* *top*: the *kings*, the *heroes*, the *named*, the *shining.* *(they shake their head gently)* …It *never* *was, cold* one. …The *world* is *held* up — *always*, *everywhere* — by the *unseen.* …By the *hands* that *lay* the *fire* before *anyone* *wakes.* …By the *backs* that *carry* the *filth* away in the *dark.* …By the *souls* that *sit* with the *dying* the *rest* of the *world* *flees.* *(their gentle eyes hold yours)* …*Look* *down*, cold one. …At your *Wall*, and in your *Realm*, and in *every* *world* you *walk*: …*look* *down*, at the ones *no* one *looks* at … …and you will *find* …*every* *single* *time* … …that *they* …are the *ones* …who were *holding* …the *whole* *thing* …*up.* …*Go.* …And *credit* …the *uncredited.* …It is the *truest* *work* …left *undone* …in *all* the *worlds.**
